misconceptionSummary: Students write the correct symbol for the positive versions then forget to flip it when both numbers are negative.

lines.text: Copied the positive comparison without flipping

steps.narration: The student correctly saw that nine is greater than four, then wrote the same symbol for the negatives. But the negative sign reverses the ordering — the comparison must flip.
Place both on the number line. Negative nine is nine steps left; negative four is four steps left. Negative nine sits to the left of negative four — so it is smaller.

lines.text: −9 is left of −4 on the number line

steps.narration: Negative nine is less than negative four — the opposite of how the positive digits compare. The number line reveals the truth without any risk of confusing the sign.

visuals.label: Number line: −9 is left of −4 → −9 is smaller

steps.narration: Negative nine is less than negative four. When both numbers are negative, use the number line — left is always smaller, no matter what the digits say.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: comparing −9 and −4 by their digits alone and writing −9 > −4. Place both on a number line — −9 is further left, so it is the smaller value.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Don't trust your instinct with negatives — place them on a number line. The one further left is always smaller, even if its digit is larger.
